sentences.text: Right.
They split us into two groups.
I was in a group that went to the OSE-- one of the OSE homes of Montmorency called Villa-- well, it makes no difference.
But it was in Montmorency.
The second group went to a different home, called Chateau Legette, which was run and financed by Baron the Rothschild, with approximately 130 children going over there.
They were both German children and Austrian children, going over there, going to Chateau Legette, as well as the OSE, there were Austrian and German-- and German kids.
Anyway, the-- the name of the house was Villa Helvetia in Montmorency, and that's where I was sent.
But again, I had already met some German children, and we started making friends.
It was-- I was very, very impressed.
I remember the first days in Montmorency, we walked into the-- we walked into this house.
And what the people had done over there, they had set up little tables, for the smaller children, for 10-year-olds.
They were our size.
And we could eat, and play, whatever it was.
But it was all colorful.
They were-- the tables were in blues and reds and yellows.
It was really very nicely done, and made us feel very much-- it's a nice place to be.
Well, it turned out it was a very nice place to be.
However, more and more children kept coming in.
And so the OSE needed more homes.
And consequently, there were two more homes set up in the outskirts of Paris.
One of them-- one of them in Eaubonne, called Villa Chesnaie, which was for the more orthodox kids.
And I was transferred from Montmorency to Eaubonne, to this particular place, where I stayed until I left, until occupation.
But the life in Eaubonne and Villa Chesnaie was really very pleasant.
We were taught.
First of all, we had to learn French.
All the kids that were there were from Germany and Austria.
They were a few, I think, from Belgium.
Most of them were-- well, in Villa Chesnaie, and in Eaubonne, it was the religious kids.
Well, we were somewhat religious at home.
But when I talk religious in Eaubonne, I mean they were ultra orthodox.
I mean, really, ultra orthodox.
It was-- I mean, it was like the hasid.
And that just wasn't me.
But you know, children want to adapt.
So I adapted to that lifestyle.
I mean, every morning it was [NON-ENGLISH], every evening it was [NON-ENGLISH].
We had Shabbats, we-- we went to synagogues.
We went to synagogues on Shabbat.
We started learning French.
They set up actually schools in the homes, themselves.
The three homes, by the way, that the OSE then had, which were Montmorency, in Eaubonne and in Soisy.
They were all in walking distance of each other-- a couple of miles possibly.
I didn't recognize it at the time, because it seemed like a shop.
Like I was the youngest kid who was in the carpenter shop.
And I learned to be-- I was basically an apprentice cabinet maker.
Most of the teachers or the counselors who were taking care of the children were also refugees from Germany and Austria-- many of them teachers, professors, doctors, what have you.
The director of the home, at the time, and it was a-- [NON-ENGLISH], his wife was a doctor.
And she was the home doctor for-- she was a GP-- for everybody.

She would give us the shots, which everybody-- I mean, any time I can remember any of the children, who I still know today, we all remember the shots they gave us in the shoulder, because nobody could move after that for about three days.
At that time, they had these DPT shots, I guess, for diphtheria and so forth.
And it was--it was painful.
But we-- we-- you know, we were organized into different rooms-- like I was in a particular room in this home with five or six other boys.
My room was room number D.
There were six other boys, all the same age group, and we started forming a real close friendship.
And basically what happened was we started becoming a family.
Instead of--instead of being friends, it was more like becoming siblings with the kids in your own room.
And that was the same thing for the other age groups, like the 12-year-olds.
Or later on, some older children started coming, who were, you know, 12, 13, 14.
Then, in approximately May of 1939, there was a ship that left Germany from Hamburg, called the St.
Louis, that took approximately 950 Jewish Jews to Cuba, who had gotten visas to enter Cuba.
Well, Cuba refused them, and turned the ship back.
And I think it's-- it's pretty much-- that's a historical event, which is known, I think, in many of the history books.
But amongst the-- eventually those people, four countries each took one quarter of the ship's population.
And the people-- the kids-- the kids who were disembarked in France, which was one of the countries that took some of the ship's population, most of those people sent their children to the OSE homes.
So we suddenly had an influx of kids that the rest of the kids immediately called the Cubans, because they came from Cuba.
They were all German kids, but we called them-- they were sort of a separate group and we called them the Cubans.
And that name stuck.

By the way, the people who disembarked from the same ship, who disembarked in England-- and those children were also sent to organizations, to a home-- we found out later that the English kids-- not the English kids, but the German kids who were-- Austrian kids who were there before-- termed them the Cubans, also.
So then, both in France and in Austria, we had this called the Cuban population, or the Cuban kids.
They were all German and Austrian.
But the kids were-- the oldest was 15.
The youngest that came was about 5 or 6.
So-- but-- number of people-- the number of kids kept increasing.
And other things that were happening is that the parents would get out and pick up the children and leave, so it made room for more kids.
So there was a constantly changing population.
Sundays was always a visitor day.
And I remember feeling very, very lonely, because I never got any visitors.
And I felt it.
I mean, there were times when I wanted to cry, because other kids got visitors and I didn't.
There were a lot of other kids that didn't get visitors, but, you know, we all felt the same thing.
There was correspondence with my parents.
We wrote letters.
I have-- I have copies of many of the letters that my parents sent me-- which I gave the originals to the Weisenthal Center, here.
They have the originals of all my documents.
So there was correspondence.
There was constant-- the correspondence constantly said, we're coming soon, we're coming soon.

And I recall one letter, which I still have a copy of, but it said, well, it looks like we're only waiting for one more papers, because it looks like we're getting a visa to the United States and we're going to settle in Los Angeles.
And it was going to happen very imminently.
Well, obviously, it never occurred.
September, 1939 is when World War II broke out, when the Germans invaded Poland.
Things started changing.
Until then, by the way, the life in Eaubonne, this home, the children's home, was very, very pleasant.
There was food.
It was-- you know, we went to-- we went to school, we had friends, we played football, we were corresponding with parents.
And the only negative that I could see is that I missed my parents, especially on visitor day when other people received visitors and I didn't.
After September, 1939, when the war broke out, things changed again.
I-- we could no longer correspond with our parents, OK, except that I was very lucky.
We had, a-- either a relative or a friend in Switzerland.
So my parents would write them, they would forward the letters to me, and I would then return letters to Switzerland to my parents.
So I was able to still correspond with them.

And so anybody who had a kind of a contact, I would take letters from some of the other children who wanted to correspond with their parents, and send it through Switzerland, so that other children were able to correspond with the parents.
And anybody who had any kind of a contact was doing this.
And of course, the counsellors recognized this, and asked the children to do this, so that's what we all did.
That's when we started.
Anyway, the war started.
And that's when we started learning about air raids, about shelters, about sandbagging, filling sandbags, and putting them in front of windows, and taping windows.
We were organized again, as far as the air raids are concerned.

When the air raids occurred, the older children would take-- would be responsible-- there was always-- would be responsible for the younger children, and the younger children were responsible for the still younger children.
So that you always had some sort of a buddy system, that you made sure that nobody would stay in the room when the air raids occurred.
We went into-- well, this particular home had a basement that was built like a fortress, with-- with curved arches, that-- I mean, it was really quite a basement.
And that's where we went.
Not only did we, the children from the home, go, but it was also an air raid shelter for some of the surrounding population, who also lived in the area.
So there were not only us, but there were other people coming out to shelter whenever air raids occurred.
Sure.
Oh, yes.
We knew the war was on.
Of course, of course.
You know, there was news all the time.
Even though school continued, even though we still played football, you know, there were always-- the rumors started coming.
Somebody saw paratroopers coming down, you know, there were talks about the fifth column with German spies coming in.
And I mean, there were all kinds of rumors floating around.
But we read newspapers, we, by that time, we had picked up enough French to be able to read newspapers.
And, of course, counsellors would tell us what was happening, anyway.
They-- the organization was, I think, very good in not trying to hide the difficulty from the children.
But primarily, the nucleus was still German and Austrian kids at the time.
No, because they kept opening more homes.
I found out later on they just opened more homes.
Now, where they got the money from, I really don't know at the time.
I'm still reading about the--about the finances.
But I do know that that Baron de Rothschild, for instance, is one of the people who was involved in-- in financing the operations.
Although he had one of the homes, Chateau Legette, he financed all by himself until he left France in 1941-- probably early '41.
And turned those kids over to the OSE, so that those homes no longer existed and it was all part of the OSE.
In a few days-- well, of course, the war continued on, and things didn't get any better obviously-- but, I mean, it was still a reasonably good life.
I remember writing to my parents, asking them for things, like would you please send me a toy, a particular thing which I wanted, or some clothes or something.
And in retrospect, I mean, I-- you know, now that I'm grown, as soon as I became more mature, I realized that that's not the thing to have done, because they were in dire straits themselves.
And they, of course, want-- want to do everything for the children.
But they thought possibly I was hurting, I was suffering, whatever it was-- which was really not the case at the time.
We also learned other disciplines over there, in addition to-- in addition to-- like I was in the carpenters' shop, cabinetmaker shop, they also had shoemakers, for instance.
We also learned how to knit and to darn socks, because a lot of things were done by ourselves.
I mean, I'd darn old socks after a while.
I was able to knit-- I knit a sweater for myself.
Most of the other kids did the same thing.
I don't recall how, as far as clothes are concerned, how we kept getting clothes, because as I get older, I must have had bigger clothes.
And I know there must have been hand me downs and so forth, but I just don't remember the details of that.
It was a fairly amount of normalcy there, right.
Well, there were other things that happened, like, for instance, one of my--my father's brother, and his wife and my cousin Otto left Austria, and fled to-- they went to Italy at the time.
And I got a card from them saying, they're leaving-- they're leaving Italy, they're going to go to Chile.
I was hoping I was able to go with them, but there just was no way.
I still have the correspondence from them.
By the way, that's the only thing I still have.
I kept correspondence, and I kept the pictures.
And I have most of those things.

I had the cards from other people, distant relatives who were leaving, who would give me the straight scoop about-- about-- for instance, my-- this couple, the cousins who had come to live at our home after-- after they'd been kicked out.
He was sent to the Dachau-- both of them were sent to Dachau at the time.
I know that others had left Austria.
They were telling me that things were getting pretty bad.

And I was only about 11 years old at the time, but I mean, I already, when I got these cards saying that, for instance, there were four families that they were telling me about-- the Eimers, who were distant cousins, the Ecklings, who were distant cousins, the Schwartzes and Hipshers-- they had left Austria and had-- those has come to the United States, for instance.
How they got visas, I don't know, but I kept getting news about it was getting worse and worse in there.
And of course, it wasn't getting better where we were, also.

Until June of 1940, when the Germans occupied Paris-- there was not more than a week or so before the Germans occupied Paris that the OSE took the kids and started sending them to other homes in-- outside of the Paris area.
Several homes were set up.
There was one home set up near Limoges, Chateau-- Chateau Montintin.
Another one near Limoges, Chateau Masgelier.
Another one near Vichy, which had already existed-- that was primarily for French kids-- in Chateau des Morelles.
At that time, I remember having a choice of going to either Chateau des Morelles, or Chateau Montintin.
We had a directrice, who was going-- who I liked, that nobody else seemed to like, but for some reason or other I liked her.
And she initially said she was going to go to Chateau des Morelles near Vichy, so I said I want to go with her.
So she put me on the list.
But then she changed her mind, and wound up-- or somebody changed their mind-- and she wound up near Chateau Montintin.
When I found that out, I asked her, I want to still go with her.
So I wound up in Chateau Montintin.
Now, Chateau Montintin was a old, old castle.
And on the outskirts-- well, it was not outskirts.
It was really the middle of a forest in Limoges-- there were farms around and so forth.
It was in the middle of a forest, though.
It was an abandoned-- it hadn't been lived in for years.
When we got there-- well, first of all, the trip to Limoges, itself, it was horrendous.
The trains were overcrowded.
Some of us were on top of the train, some were inside the trains.
I mean, people were hanging on to it.

Some of the older kids-- those who were 14, 15, and 16-- couldn't even get on the trains, but we had one old truck there that one of the people would take them and go down to Chateau Montintin by this old truck.
Some of the kids were riding bicycles.
It was-- it was just horrendous.
But when we did get to Chateau Montintin--
Approximately, it was about five or six days.
Yeah, it was-- it was quite a while, because the trains kept stopping.
There were-- there were bombings.
Then we got to stations where the station had been bombed out, and they had to change trains.
And it-- it was just horrendous.
On the road, you saw the effects of the war.
You saw-- this is first time I'd seen dead people on the road.
And you know-- it was-- the roads were just crowded with people, just trying to get anywhere, get out.
It was-- it was just a horrendous experience.
And of course, there was a shortage of food.
It was the first time we experienced hunger.
But we did get the Chateau Montintin.
It was a very-- like an old chateau.
The place was completely empty, not a stick of furniture-- nothing, OK?
All the kids, the counsellors, through the organization, we started scrounging.
The boys from the-- from the cabinetmaker shop, they started getting tools in there.
We started making benches and tables and somehow they scrounged up beds.
But after about three or four months, we all had beds to sleep in.
We didn't have to sleep on the floors anymore.
In Chateau Montintin, they had an sort of an ancillary house, which was removed by, maybe, oh, an eighth of a mile.
Well, it was within a five-minute walk.
It was a little house, and they called it Le Chevrette, which means a little goat, actually.
And the kids from the orthodox home, from Eaubonne, were placed in La Chevrette.
And the kids from the other homes, which were the less orthodox, or the non-orthodox, were placed in the Chateau Montintin.
But the whole complex was called Chateau Montintin.
I was in the house, which had four bedrooms for the boys, and three bedrooms for the girls.
There was an average of six people to each room.
So there were approximately 40 of us sleeping in this one little house.
And there, the life, again, changed very, very radically.
The food was very sparse.
We were pretty much always hungry.
Meat was nonexistent.
There was a vegetable, which is sort of a turnip, called [FRENCH], which the French farmers used to feed to their pigs, but we ate a lot of those.
I mean, we ate what we call [FRENCH], which is sort of a grass type, a leafy type grass.
We had one of the-- excuse me-- the counselors, who came there, by the way, were in the same boat that we were.
They were all refugees.

OK, they still working for the OSE but at least-- when were in Chateau Montintin, the demarcation line between the, quote, 'free France' and unoccupied France and occupied France, we were in the unoccupied zone.
That doesn't mean that there weren't any Germans there, because the Germans started coming around even in the unoccupied zones.
And they started picking up kids, you know.
When they got to be 16, the-- kids started disappearing.
They had to register with the police, and so forth.
The function of the OSE actually changed during that period of time.
It was a very open organization before.
It started becoming more clandestine, like they tried to-- well, it even got-- they did a lot more later on, after I left.
But what they tried to do is they tried to get the kids out.
Because they recognized that the-- that the-- the danger were the Jewish kids.
And in France, the French government, of course, didn't help any, because the French government was really-- they turned over the Jews before the-- even the Germans asked for them.
And the first ones that turned over is the, quote, 'the foreign Jews,' which include, of course, our group of kids of German, and Austrians, and Belgians, and so forth.
So those kids were in the most imminent danger.
Montintin, yeah?
Yes.
Yes.
Because once I got to Montintin, I was able to write to Switzerland.
Yes, yes.
Now, while I was in Montintin, one of the letters I got from my parents was that had been-- they had moved to a different address in Vienna.
Which, at the time, I recognized that basically they were kicked out and put into a sort of-- they had to sublet a room in Vienna.
And shortly thereafter, they were deported.
I did not hear from them for a while.
No, I actually found out they were deported, because once they were deported, they were deported-- they were not sent to a concentration camp.
They would deport Jews at the time to Poland, to anywhere, just to get them out of Germany or Austria.
My parents were deported beginning of 1941.
With my brother, right.
They were sent to a town in Poland called Opole, O-P-O-L-E.
I have subsequently found out, providing through the Austrian government, as to what-- I knew exactly what transport was, because I found that out after the war by doing a certain amount of research.
But once they arrived there, they were able to still communicate to Switzerland.
So I was still able to receive letters, and that's how I know they were deported from Austria into Poland.
We're approximately at the beginning of 1941, January, February, when they were deported.
I think the first letter I started getting again was in March or April of 1941.
And then I started communicating with them again.
At first, when they were deported, my father would write, and my mother would always add something on.
And my brother would write a few words also.
After a while, I-- my brother no longer wrote anything on there.
And the last letter I got from my parents, which was on the day of my bar mitzvah after I came to the United States, which I will-- I will read it later on-- only my father wrote.
So I am very positive that my father-- my brother and my mother were already dead at the time.
And my father must have been killed shortly afterwards.
What I did find out from-- from the Austrian government, that those Jews who were deported to this town of Opole, were left basically to-- for starvation.
They just let them starve to death.
And those who did not get starved to death, that those who did not starve to death, there were mass shootings.
And any survivors of that were sent to the Sobibor concentration camp.
Sure.
I will translate the letter-- I have translated the letter into English.

It was written on October 19th, 1941 which is the day of my 13th birthday. 'My dearest love, today, on your birthday, as it was your day of your bar mitzvah, we all-- that is, dearest mother, your brother Fritz, and I-- are sitting in a tiny, tiny room, and are constantly both thinking and talking about you.
Our total longing thoughts are only addressed at you, and you alone.
And our deepest wish is to be able to be together with you again in peace and joy.
How we inwardly, my dear [NON-ENGLISH], are longing to hear any news from you or about you, I cannot express in words.
But the child's heart alone must somehow reflect our total longing for you.
Before anything else, we long to know where you are-- if in an orphanage, or by your Uncle Sigmund, and Aunt Ana.
Furthermore, if you are healthy, how you look, how you feel, and your surroundings.
And now, my golden-haired-- and now, my golden-haired youngest son, I give you my innermost congratulations and blessings on this, your most important and solemn day.

May your luck shine, and be as bright as the stars in heaven, and may it be written in our future to be able to hold you in our arms and make life again nicer for you, as we have always strived to do.
You alone are our total longing and hope.
And therefore, you must remain healthy, big, and strong.
In my thoughts, I lift my hands over your head, and bless you with a blessing from the Bible.
May God bless you, shelter, and protect you, and always hold his right hand over you.
Amen.
We are healthy, well.
Again, with the most urgent wishes to see you again in peace and joy, I embrace you and kiss you many thousand times.
Your always faithful, Papa.' It's a very moving letter, now that I-- now that I'm grown.
At the time, I did not recognize all the implications of it, but it was probably the last letter that he's ever written.
Well, that was written, of course, after I came to the United States.
In the meantime, I was still in France, and life continued over there, at Chateau Montintin.
We-- we did go to school.
My carpentry skills improved.
We started learning English.
And what the OSE tried to do, is they tried getting the children out.
Which, I think I've said it before.
In the meantime, amongst the things that have occurred, the-- in the United States, there was a movement to get some of the children to the-- to the United States.

The joint committee, along with Eleanor Roosevelt, the American Quakers, and, from Chicago, there was the magnate, Marshall Fields-- somehow, I'm reading history, but those are the organizations that were responsible of trying to get children to the United States.
The initial plan had been to send-- to pick up basically 1,000 of these stray kids, and bring them to the United States.
They were successful in getting 200 over here.
There were basically two transports.
There may have been a third one later on that I'm reading about now, but I'm not 100% sure of this one.

From what I've read, the way they tried to pick the children, they tried to pick the kids who would have the least chance of survival in France-- which means the foreign-born kids, not the French kids.
If you had relatives in France, the chances are you could not go.
If your parents have been deported, the chances are that you would be put on the list.
I was picked, along with all the other kids in my particular room at Montintin, to be on the first transport of 100 children.
For some reason or other, the transport left in about May-- about May of 1941.

The children were assembled, not only from Chateau Montintin, but also from some of the other homes, like Masgelier, and-- let's see, there was Masgelier, there was Chateau Chabanne-- there were several other homes.
They were all sent to Marseilles.
And from there on in, the Quakers did a lot of paperwork and processing the paperwork, so, to get the-- to get the kids out.
And 100 children, then, arrived in the United States June 22nd, 1941.
And that was the first transport.
Now, I was supposed to go on the first transport.
I had been picked for it, myself.
And, for some reason or other, approximately a week before the transport was supposed to leave, I was told I'm not going.
I've been taken off the transport-- myself and another girl.
A girl by the name of Ruth Drucker.
